dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 with RFD for a priority 1 breathing problem.
Good morning, sir. I'm Rachel, an EMT. What brought you to call 911 today?
I can't catch my breath, and it's been going on for two days now, got worse last night.
Let me check your alertness. What's your name?
James, James Thompson.
Mr. Thompson, what is the date today?
It is Saturday, the 8th.
I want you to squeeze my fingers. I'm outside your right hand now.
Okay, you're warm.
Your airway is patent and clear. Are you able to speak comfortably?
Let's look at your breathing. Your respirations are slightly rapid. Are you still experiencing trouble filling your lungs?
Your breathing sounds reveal expiratory wheezing bilaterally in upper and lower lobes.
Respirations are rapid at 22 breaths per minute and of normal depth.
Pulse is 87, strong and regular. Skin is warm, dry, and of normal color.
Radial pulse is 87 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 173 over 102 millimeters of mercury.
Oxygen saturation is 91 percent at baseline without supplemental oxygen.
The 4-lead EKG shows sinus rhythm with no abnormalities detected.
A glucose reading is 128 mg/dL.
What medications have you been taking, and did they help with the shortness of breath?
I use glypizide and albuterol. I tried the inhaler, but it didn't help.
Do you have any chronic health issues or diagnosed medical conditions?
I have COPD, high blood pressure, diabetes, and I've been managed for depression and anxiety.
When was the last time you had food or drink?
I had some tea and a sandwich around 10 AM. Not much after that.
What were you doing just before you decided to call 911?
I hoped the flare would pass at home, but I just can't breathe like I normally should.
Have you experienced any pain associated with your breathing or anywhere else in your body today?
No, I don't have any pain, just severe shortness of breath.
Does the feeling feel like pressure, tightening, or something else in your chest or elsewhere?
No, the discomfort is all in my lungs, like I can't get enough air in or out quickly enough.
Are you feeling no pain, and if you were to rate your discomfort on a scale of 1-10, how would you rate it right now?
I feel no pain per se, but my relief would be a 10. I'm struggling to breathe, not in physical pain otherwise. I'd say 2 on the scale.
We're placing you on 4 lead EKG monitoring to assess for any cardiac changes during the exacerbation.
Starting nebulizer therapy with 5 mg albuterol and 0.5 mg atrovent via DuoNeb with 8 L oxygen to help reduce bronchospasm.
Supporting you with this nebulizer treatment now. You should feel some relief shortly; let me know if you notice any improvement or side effects.
A 12-lead EKG was performed; we see sinus rhythm, no ST segment concerns-no cardiac-related changes detected.
IV placed successfully in the left forearm for medication administration and fluid support if needed later.
Giving you 10 mg dexamethasone via the IV line to address the inflammation contributing to your airway narrowing. It should reduce the worsening trend of your COPD symptoms on site.
Following protocol 3-11 for COPD exacerbation based on our assessment and no improvement so far with initial treatment combinations.
Pulse is 86, regular. Respirations have decreased to 19 per minute and are smoother in depth.
Blood pressure is 159 over 97. Oxygen saturation is up to 95%-improvement from 91%.
Starting second nebulizer treatment with 5 mg albuterol now to continue bronchodilation efforts.
SpO2 is 99-100% on the nebulizer. Glucose remains 0, but it is not of immediate concern for your breathing distress; the hospital will document that for their report.
We're still hearing wheezing on expiration-diffuse wheezing per the original findings. No appreciable change yet.
We will be taking you to VCU Medical Center for further treatment as this is a known COPD exacerbation with incomplete response to EMS field interventions.
We're securing you to the stretcher using a 5-point harness now for a stable transport.
Hospital transport is necessary in cases of unresolved breathing distress. You need pulmonary assessment and possibly non-invasive ventilation per hospital protocol.
Providing a full report to the nurse upon arrival; ongoing oxygen and nebulizer treatment. A repeat of your pulse oximetry and your vitals are showing initial improvement.
Mr. Thompson is now with Bed 13 staff in the ED, and care has been transferred into the hands of the ED team. They have initiated treatment options as appropriate.
It's hard for me to take a deep inhale without panicking. I still feel like I am not getting enough air out of my lungs.
Let's check your alertness again. What's the year today?
What city are you in right now?
I am in Richmond, Virginia.
Squeeze my fingers with your left hand now please.
Done. Your gloves are slippery but sturdy.
Your airway remains patent and unobstructed. You're speaking clearly and keeping it clear.
Breathing is still slightly labored but you're clearly aware and following our instructions.
SpO2 is currently at 95% with continual monitoring set to continue automatically.
Blood pressure is 159 over 97-trend downward from 173 over 102. Stable for transport.
Your skin is normal in color, no cyanosis, and there is no sign of tissue perfusion difficulties such as mottling or pallor.
Your neck veins are not distended. You are not hoarse or having trouble swallowing per recent report.
I don't see or hear any obstruction in your airway.
Your breath sounds are still showing wheezing in the upper and lower lobes bilaterally-no asymmetry detected.
Your abdomen is soft, and you're not guarding or showing any other signs of acute illness documented.
You have no reported complaints of urinary frequency or urgency.
Your legs are free from swelling, and you can move them easily.
Your pulse remains at 86 beats per minute and remains regular.
Respiratory efforts are 19 per minute and are now slowing further under the influence of the second nebulizer run.
Your blood glucose is 0-your home blood sugar management shows this is your baseline, and we will note it for hospital reference.
The EKG continues to show sinus rhythm, no changes noted. No new ST elevation or rhythm issues observed.
Dexamethasone has been successfully delivered by IV push with flushing for complete delivery.
Albuterol remains the second medication given in the past 13 minutes with no adverse events or contraindications.
A new 12-lead EKG was not necessary at this stage, but otherwise everything from earlier is on record.
You mentioned feeling a level 2 for breathing distress. Has it changed or improved?
It has improved slightly. I can now complete a full sentence without gasping for air-is it downhill from here?
No chest pain, no new discomfort, so we'll continue to treat based on current assessment numbers. We'll reassess if symptoms worsen.
Any new onset of nausea, dizziness, or pale appearance as we come closer to transport?
No, my symptoms are stable. No signs of change or other new issues.
Hospital transport has been secured. Preparations for your bed assignment at VCU are in progress-expect arrival soon.
You are now fully secured and ready for a non-emergent transfer.
At the hospital, they are preparing a bed for you to ensure no delay once we arrive.
Your medications, airway, breathing, and vital signs are stable at this moment. They have a repeat visual check before receiving you at the door.
We provided documentation like the nebulizer therapy, IV access, and chronic conditions that they need to reference upon intake.
The nurse on duty has read your chart, and we are in good shape for immediate handover.
No additional interventions are required. You're currently stable and well-managed with our treatment.
Your mental status remains intact-still alert, oriented, following directions at 4 of 4. GCS is 15, no deviation from initial assessment.
No new findings on repeat systems check-print data has been gathered in preparation for hospital entry.
EKG remains uneventful, still in sinus rhythm as observed on the dedicated 12-lead report.
Transport in progress, speed is maintained to align with your non-emergent status.
We have ongoing oxygen via the nebulizer and mask as we approach the facility to ensure secondary baseline can be maintained if needed.
Hospital staff are on standby and will document all of our findings as we transfer care.
All field protocols have been followed. You will be handed over to Bed 13 in the ER.
I will be providing a verbal summary and a written patient care report to the nurse.
Our final SpO2 batch read was 99-100% during treatment. For transport, we're maintaining oxygen saturation close to baseline per your regular needs.
Blood sugar remains at 0 for their records over the course. No action is performed for BG in current context.
Your VCU chart entry is complete. The lab will handle any glucose discrepancy if encountered post-arrival.
No chest pain or any new complaints have emerged during the current assessment cycle or during past cycles.
We requested documentation confirmation from patient earlier. No changes reported thus far.
No further input into procedure protocols at this time. We've supported you with all possible tools.
Stretcher ride is smooth as we cross into the ER parking lot. No loss of gear or fluctuation in condition.
All interventions are noted and updated. Final IV status is stable. No issues with line patency and flushes.
We also noted no complication from the medications administered. Side effects were not noticed in field.
Arriving to Bed 13 per initial protocol. You will be lifted off stretcher and placed on bed as needed.
All straps released as we now transition from stretcher to the bed.
Staff RN has full report and full record for hospital integration.
Airway, breath, and blood pressure management are within expected limits for now.
All transfers complete. You're now promptly in the hands of hospital management.
If you have any more questions, directing you to the hospital team for appropriate handover.
